rescue_reasoning: Keep because climate relevance is explicit via the parent statement: "Corporate Climate Change Adaptation Plan (CCCAP)... actions to adapt City assets, operations, and services to climate change impacts." The sub-action "Investigate the feasibility of a backwater valve program" is a typical flood/sewer-backup risk reduction measure consistent with adaptation, even if the sub-action lacks explicit outcome tokens. Climate screen = explicit_parent breaks the tie in favor of inclusion.
policy_statement: Investigate the feasibility of a backwater valve program.
mechanism_description: The city investigates a backwater valve program to help reduce sewer backup and indoor flooding risk during heavy rainfall events.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall → sewer surcharge) → ↑ protective measure adoption (backwater valves) → ↓ exposure/impact (sewer backups, indoor flooding)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city evaluates (and may design) a program that enables/encourages installation of backwater valves on private laterals/building plumbing. (2) Process: During high inflow/infiltration or storm-driven surcharging, the valve prevents reverse flow of wastewater/stormwater into basements, reducing flood pathway connectivity between sewers and buildings. (3) Outcome: Lower frequency/severity of sewer backups and indoor flooding damages and associated health risks under increasing extreme precipitation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced indoor flooding/sewer-backup impacts during extreme rainfall by lowering building exposure to sewer surcharge.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: managing climate-driven heavy rainfall impacts by reducing building exposure to sewer surcharge via backwater valves. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: while there can be minor co-benefits (e.g., reduced cleanup energy/materials), the core intent and largest effect is flood risk reduction, which is Adaptation rather than Mitigation/Resource Efficiency/Nature-Based Solutions. 3) Edge cases applied: the policy here is a feasibility assessment rather than direct installation, but it is still part of an adaptation pathway because it enables a protective program aimed at hazard impact reduction. 4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy sharing this mechanism (feasibility/assessment of backwater valve programs) primarily targets sewer-backup/indoor-flooding risk reduction under heavy rainfall. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “flood management,” “building resilience,” “stormwater/sewer operations”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “backwater valve program” were framed mainly as reducing combined sewer overflows to waterways (water quality compliance) rather than protecting buildings, the dominant pathway could shift in interpretation; however, for sewer-backup/indoor-flooding risk reduction it is squarely Adaptation. Feasibility-only actions have indirect impacts but remain categorized by intended dominant pathway.
co_benefits: Reduced sewage contamination and associated public health risks; reduced property damage and insurance claims
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as an incentive-program if the text indicated rebates/subsidies or implementation; however, the wording is limited to feasibility investigation, so it is best treated as planning/strategy.
